summary: Tight set with scorching Scarlet>Fire and soaring Bird Song
blurb: The show delivers a blistering Scarlet Begonias → Fire on the Mountain and a soaring Bird Song. Jerry’s guitar and vocals are in top form, while Brent’s keyboards add a sexy edge. Overall the performance is tight, energetic, and one of the hotter shows of ’89.
review:
On April 16, 1989 at The MECCA, the Dead rolled out a concise, well‑paced set that kept the momentum humming from start to finish. The first set opened with a raucous Hell In A Bucket into They Love Each Other before diving into a gritty Little Red Rooster and a snarling Stagger Lee. Highlights piled up with Mama Tried, Mexicali Blues and an inspired Bird Song that had Jerry’s voice soaring and his guitar hitting those high‑note peaks.

The second set is where the night really ignites: a smoking Scarlet Begonias → Fire on the Mountain sequence that reviewers call “smoking” and “tight,” followed by a solid Saint of Circumstance and a driving Truckin'. The rhythm section locks in with pounding drums, ringing xylophone‑like tones, and space that launches into an expansive The Other One. Stella Blue and Throwing Stones keep the energy high before a heartfelt Turn On Your Lovelight and a beautiful Box of Rain encore. Brent Mydland’s raspy, sexy keyboards sit atop the mix, and Phil’s massive bass line “bombs the Mecca,” while both drummers stay creative yet solid throughout.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Four of five recordings are clearly positive, praising the tight playing, standout songs (Scarlet>Fire, Bird Song, Stagger Lee, The Other One) and overall energy; one mixed review adds no negative consensus. This strong positive sentiment yields a +1.6 adjustment.

best_recording.reason: Excellent soundboard capture with clear, crisp mix; preserves the tight performance and highlights while fixing earlier thinness issues.

key_highlights: Scorching Scarlet Begonias → Fire on the Mountain sequence
Soaring Bird Song with standout vocals and guitar
Tight rhythm section powering The Other One and Throwing Stones
Brent’s raspy, sexy keyboards lift the whole band

band_performance.Jerry: Brilliant guitar tone and powerful vocals throughout; scales high notes on Hell and delivers a soaring Bird Song, all‑there with no noodling.
band_performance.Phil: Massive, driving bass that "bombed the Mecca," providing a solid foundation for the tight set.
band_performance.Bob: Belting lyrics with crunchy, delicious guitar fills; strong vocal harmonies with Jerry throughout the show.
band_performance.Brent: Raspy, sexy keyboard work on Rooster and everywhere else; praised as being at his absolute best, lifting the entire band.
band_performance.Mickey: Drum segment described as pounding with ringing xylophone‑like tones, adding a cinematic feel to the jams.
band_performance.Billy: Solid beatkeeping while staying creative and adventurous, complementing Mickey’s textures.
